title: What 'Generative' Really Requires
concept: A true generative model needs a latent space where ANY sampled point decodes to something plausible, not just memorized training points.

exercises.prompt: If you shuffle a deck of memorized flashcards and pick a random one, are you 'creating' anything new?

options.text: No — you're just recalling something that already existed
Yes — shuffling makes it brand new
Yes — because you closed your eyes when picking

exercises.explain: Picking from a fixed set isn't creation — it's retrieval. Real generation means making something that was never stored in the first place.

cards.title: Is It Really 'Generating'?
cards.body: Imagine an AI that draws amazing cat pictures... but only ever redraws the exact 100 cats it was trained on. Cool trick — but is it truly 'generative'? Today we find out what generative models actually need to earn that name.

cards.title: What Makes a Model Truly Generative
cards.body: A generative model learns a 'latent space' — a map of hidden possibilities. For it to be truly generative, ANY point you randomly pick from that space should decode into something new and plausible, not just spit back a memorized training example.

cards.title: The Recipe Book vs. The Chef
cards.body: A model that only memorizes is like a photo album — it can only show you pictures it already has. A true generative model is like a chef who understands flavors so well they can cook a brand-new dish you never tasted before, using the same ingredients.

cards.title: Sampling a New Face
cards.body: In a well-trained face-generating model, if you pick a totally random point in latent space — one that was never used during training — you should still get a realistic-looking face. If instead you got static noise or a broken image, the model hasn't really learned the 'space' of faces, just a few isolated dots.

cards.title: Myth: Memorizing Training Data = Being Generative
cards.body: Nope! A model that just stores and replays training examples isn't generative — it's a database. True generative power means covering the whole space smoothly, so even unseen points make sense.

cards.title: Memorized Dots vs. Smooth Space
cards.body: Picture latent space as a map. One model only has usable land at a few marked dots (memorized points). The other has an entire smooth continent you can land anywhere on.

left.text: Only a few scattered dots work — everywhere else is blank or broken

right.text: The whole space is filled in — any point gives something plausible

exercises.prompt: What does a truly generative model need in its latent space?

options.text: Only the exact points used during training need to work
Any randomly sampled point should decode into something plausible
A giant lookup table of every training example
No latent space at all — just memory

exercises.explain: The whole point of 'generative' is generalizing beyond the training data — any sampled point should still make sense.

exercises.prompt: A model that only reproduces its exact training examples counts as truly generative.
exercises.explain: That's just memorization, not generation. True generative models must handle new, unseen points smoothly.

exercises.sentence: A true generative model fills its latent space so that ___ sampled point decodes into something plausible.
exercises.explain: 'Any' is key — plausibility should hold everywhere in the space, not just at memorized spots.

exercises.prompt: You sample a random point from a model's latent space and get pure garbled noise instead of a coherent image. What does this suggest about the model?

options.text: It has likely just memorized isolated points instead of learning a smooth, meaningful space
It is a perfect generative model
The random number generator is broken

exercises.explain: Garbled output from a random point signals gaps in the latent space — a sign of memorization rather than true generalization.

exercises.prompt: An AI art model was trained on 500 paintings. When you give it the exact same 500 'codes' used in training, it makes gorgeous paintings. But any other code produces meaningless blobs. Is this a good generative model?

options.text: Yes, because the paintings it makes are gorgeous
No, because it only works at memorized points, not across the whole latent space
Yes, because it has 500 examples, which is a lot
No, because paintings should never be gorgeous

exercises.explain: Quality at only the training points is a memorization red flag. A real generative model must produce plausible results from any point, proving it learned the underlying structure — not just the answers.
